title: Morph: Efficient File-Lifetime Redundancy Management for Cluster File Systems
abstract: Many data services tune and change redundancy configu- rations of files over their lifetimes to address changes in data temperature and latency requirements. Unfortunately, changing redundancy configs (transcode) is IO-intensive. The Morph cluster file system introduces new transcode-efficient redundancy schemes to minimize overheads as files progress through lifetime phases. For newly ingested data, commonly stored via 3-way replication, Morph introduces a hybrid re- dundancy scheme that combines a replica with an erasure- coded (EC) stripe, reducing both ingest IO and capacity over- heads while enabling free transcode to EC by deleting repli- cas. For subsequent transcodes to wider, more space-efficient EC configs, Morph exploits Convertible Codes, which mini- Figure 1. Morph reduces ingest+transcode IO on real-world cluster data. mize data read for EC transcode, and introduces new block The graphs on the left show one week of measured hourly ingest+transcode placement policies to maximize their effectiveness. IO (top=A) and transcode-only IO (bottom=C) for one of the largest Google Analysis of data ingest and transcode activity in Google data services. The graphs on the right show the IO to provide the same data storage clusters shows the current massive IO load and the protections and lifetime transitions using Morph. The colors categorize IO for the different lifetime transitions: ingest, transcode from 3-way replica- potential savings from Morph’s approach—transcode IO can tion to a narrow EC (e.g., RS(8,12)) after a day or so, transcode from narrow be reduced by over 95%, and total ingest+transcode IO can be EC to medium LRC (e.g., LRC(32,38)) after a month or so, and transcode reduced by 50–60% while also reducing capacity overheads from medium LRC to wide LRC (e.g.,LRC(64,74)) after several months. for newly ingested data by 20%. title: Convertible Codes: Enabling Efficient Conversion of Coded Data in Distributed Storage
abstract: Erasure codes are essential for providing efficient resilience against node failures in distributed storage. Typically, an [n, k] erasure code encodes k symbols into n symbols which are then stored in different nodes. Recent work by Kadekodi et al. shows that the failure rates of storage nodes vary significantly over time, and that changing the rate of the code (via a change in n and k) in response to such variations provides substantial storage space savings. However, the resource overhead of re-encoding the already encoded data is prohibitively Fig. 1. title: A Survey of the Past, Present, and Future of Erasure Coding for Storage Systems
EC-Store: Bridging the Gap Between Storage and Latency in Distributed Erasure Coded Systems

title: Identifying Hot and Cold Data in Main-Memory Databases
Cluster Storage Systems Gotta Have HeART: Improving Storage Efficiency by Exploiting Disk-Reliability Heterogeneity
abstract: Large-scale cluster storage systems typically consist of a heterogeneous mix of storage devices with significantly varying failure rates. Despite such differences among de- vices, redundancy settings are generally configured in a one- scheme-for-all fashion. In this paper, we make a case for exploiting reliability heterogeneity to tailor redundancy set- tings to different device groups. We present HeART, an online tuning tool that guides selection of, and transitions Figure 1: Annualized failure rate (AFR) for the six disk groups between redundancy settings for long-term data reliability, that make up >90% of the 100,000+ HDDs used for the Backblaze based on observed reliability properties of each disk group. backup service [5]. title: Practical Design Considerations for Wide Locally Recoverable Codes (LRCs)
abstract: Most of the data in large-scale storage clusters is erasure coded. At exascale, optimizing erasure codes for low storage overhead, efficient reconstruction, and easy deployment is of critical importance. Locally recoverable codes (LRCs) have deservedly gained central importance in this field, because they can balance many of these requirements. In our work we study wide LRCs; LRCs with large number of blocks per stripe and low storage overhead. These codes are a natural next step for practitioners to unlock higher storage savings, but they come with their own challenges. Of particular inter- Figure 1: We captured a sample of 278 unavailable stripes captured est is their reliability, since wider stripes are prone to more from four Google storage clusters, along with information about the simultaneous failures. exact block failures in each sample. title: HSM: A Hybrid Storage Method Based on the Heat of Data and Global Disk Space Utilization
abstract: In distributed systems, the method for data storage is crucial. Previous data storage work use the replication or Erasure Coding method to store data. Such single storage method leads to the excessive storage overheads for cold data with low access frequency or the low reading performance for hot data with high access frequency. Nowadays, the research on the hybrid storage has become a hot topic of concern for many scholars. Existing hybrid storage works take into account data reading performance and the storage overheads, and use the replication and Erasure Coding methods to store the hot data and cold data respectively. However, in the scenarios of sufficient disk space or low disk space, these fixed data storage methods will lead to the relatively low system data reading performance or the excessively low disk space of the system. In this paper, we propose HSM, a hybrid storage method based on the heat of data and global disk space utilization. HSM fully considers the system’s requirements for the data reading performance and storage overheads under different global disk space utilization scenarios, and adaptively selects appropriate storage methods for data whose heat is different through data deletion, data reconstruction, and data archiving. The experiment results show that when system disk space is sufficient, HSM reduces data reading time by up to 18%; when system disk space is low, although increasing storage overhead by up to 7%, HSM reduces cross-rack data transfer traffic by up to 20% and cross-rack data transfer time by up to 15% compared with ERP in the process of changing the storage methods.

title: ER-Store: A Hybrid Storage Mechanism with Erasure Coding and Replication in Distributed Database Systems
abstract: In distributed database systems, as cluster scales grow, eﬃciency and availability become critical considerations. In a cluster, a common approach to high availability is using replication, but this is ineﬃcient due to its low storage utilization. Erasure coding can provide data reliability while ensuring high storage utilization. However, due to the large number of coding and decoding operations required by the CPU, it is not suitable for some frequently updated data. In order to optimize the storage eﬃciency of the data in the distributed system without aﬀecting the availability of the data, this paper proposes a data temperature recognition algorithm that can distinguish data tablets and divides data tablets into three types, cold, warm, and hot, according to the frequency of access. Combining three replicas and erasure coding technology, ER-store is proposed, a hybrid storage mechanism for diﬀerent data types. At the same time, we combined the read-write separation architecture of the distributed database system to design the data temperature conversion cycle, which reduces the computational overhead caused by frequent updates of erasure coding technology. We have implemented this design on the CBase database system based on the read-write separation ar- chitecture, and the experimental results show that it can save 14.6%–18.3% of the storage space while meeting the eﬃcient access performance of the system. 1. title: HyRES: A Hybrid Replication and Erasure Coding Approach to Data Storage
abstract: Reliability in distributed storage systems has typi- cally focused on the design and deployment of data replication Replication (R=3) or erasure coding techniques. Although some scenarios have 1.0 Reed Solomon Code (14,10) Repair Rate as fraction of Original File considered the use of replication for hot data and erasure XORBAS (16,10) coding for cold data in the same system, each is designed in HyRES (2,2,1,10) isolation. We propose HyRES, a hybrid scheme incorporates the 0.8 HyRES (2,3,0,10) arXiv:2511.00896v1 [cs.IT] 2 Nov 2025 best characteristics of each scheme, thus, resulting in additional design flexibility and better potential performance for the system. HyRES (2,2,2,10) We show that HyRES generalizes previously proposed hybrid MSR (14,10) schemes. We characterize the theoretical performance of HyRES 0.6 MBR (14,10) as well as that of replication and erasure coding considering the effects of the size of the storage networks. We validate our theoretical results using simulations. These results show 0.4 that HyRES can yield simultaneously lower storage costs than replication, lower probabilities of file loss than replication and Only 13% higher repair than replication erasure coding with similar worst case performance, and even lower effective repair traffic than replication when considering 0.2 the network size.

title: Towards Benchmarking Erasure Coding Schemes in Object Storage System: A Systematic Review
abstract: Keywords: Erasure Coding (EC) is a security measure that eliminates the need for complete data replication by Erasure Coding allowing data to be reconstructed from parity pieces. This method offers increased data redundancy, OpenStack Swift efficiency, lowered storage costs, and enhanced fault tolerance making it a preferred alternative to Reed-Solomon Codes replication in Object Storage Systems. The fundamental concept behind EC is to encrypt a specific tp Cloud Storage System amount of data in a manner that guarantees the transfer of all coded pieces without any loss. As data volumes continue to rapidly increase, the time efficiency of the EC method becomes crucial in ensuring optimal system performance. Various variables, including the algorithm employed, data size, number of storage nodes, hardware resources, and network conditions, can influence the speed of EC operations. Hence, we inspect the Erasure Coding algorithm known as Reed-Solomon Codes (RS). We ee explore the algorithm’s encoding speed, taking into account factors such as data size and the generation of parity blocks. By evaluating and enhancing existing mechanisms, our research aims to address time efficiency and fault tolerance challenges in cloud-based object storage systems. To enhance data retrieval and storage processes, we comprehensively examine time efficiency mechanisms, such as data placement policies and scheduling algorithms. Additionally, we investigate the time taken for rr an open-source cloud storage system, OpenStack Swift, to store data by analyzing two datasets and determining the duration required to store these files. Furthermore, our study assesses fault tolerance mechanisms, including redundancy schemes, error correction codes, and distributed data placement strategies, to bolster system resilience. We propose different approaches to minimize access latency, improve overall time efficiency, and ensure data availability, even in the presence of failures.

title: Enabling Efficient and Reliable Transition from Replication to Erasure Coding for Clustered File Systems
abstract: To balance performance and storage efficiency, clustered file systems often store new data with replication and later encode it with erasure coding. This paper argues that random replica placement harms both performance and availability during the subsequent encoding step, and proposes encoding-aware replication that reduces cross-rack downloads, avoids post-encoding relocation, and preserves load balancing. HDFS-based experiments and simulations show substantial throughput gains over random replication.

title: PACEMAKER: Avoiding HeART attacks in storage clusters with disk-adaptive redundancy
abstract: Disk-adaptive redundancy can reduce storage overhead by tailoring redundancy to observed device failure rates, but naive transitions between schemes can overwhelm cluster I/O for long periods. PACEMAKER studies this transition overload on production traces from Google, NetApp, and Backblaze and proposes a low-overhead orchestrator that proactively organizes layouts and schedules transitions safely. The paper shows that transition I/O can be reduced to at most 5% of cluster bandwidth while still achieving meaningful space savings.

title: Efficient and Available In-memory KV-Store with Hybrid Erasure Coding and Replication
abstract: Cocytus studies how to combine erasure coding and replication in an in-memory key-value store without losing availability. The system uses replication for small and scattered metadata while applying erasure coding only to larger values, and adds an online recovery protocol to avoid long service interruption. Implemented in Memcached, Cocytus saves 33% to 46% memory compared with primary-backup replication while keeping latency and throughput degradation small.

title: Tiger: Disk-Adaptive Redundancy Without Placement Restrictions
abstract: Tiger revisits disk-adaptive redundancy and removes the placement restrictions imposed by earlier systems that partition disks into homogeneous subclusters. It proposes a disk-adaptive redundancy system that keeps good space savings while improving risk diversity and robustness to changes in device failure behavior. The paper is useful as a modern reference on system-state-aware redundancy management beyond one-scheme-fits-all cluster designs.

title: CBase-EC: Achieving Optimal Throughput-Storage Efficiency Trade-Off Using Erasure Codes
abstract: CBase-EC targets distributed databases with read-write separation that need high throughput for hot tablets and better storage efficiency for colder data. It proposes a hot/cold tablet recognition algorithm, a bidirectional hot-to-cold and cold-to-hot conversion algorithm, and LRC-based optimizations for encoding and updates. On CBase, it improves storage efficiency by 18.4% while keeping transaction performance degradation within 6% relative to three replicas.

title: GreenHDFS: Towards An Energy-Conserving, Storage-Efficient, Hybrid Hadoop Compute Cluster
abstract: GreenHDFS introduces hot and cold zones in a Hadoop cluster and uses data-classification-driven placement so cold-zone servers can stay idle long enough to enter energy-saving modes. Using Yahoo! traces, it shows that temperature-aware placement and migration policies can preserve hot-zone performance while reducing energy use by about 26%. The paper is useful as a practical reference for self-adaptive hot/cold placement rather than redundancy conversion itself.

title: Janus: Optimal Flash Provisioning for Cloud Storage Workloads
abstract: Janus studies workload-aware tiering in a cloud-scale distributed file system with flash and disk tiers. It characterizes cacheability from sampled traces, formulates flash allocation as an optimization problem, and recommends FIFO or LRU style migration policies per workload. In production evaluations, the recommended allocations improve flash hit rate by 47% to 76%, making it a strong policy-level reference for temperature-aware placement decisions.

title: HaRD: A Heterogeneity-Aware Replica Deletion for HDFS
abstract: HaRD focuses on the often-overlooked cost of decreasing replication factors in HDFS when data popularity drops. It shows that naive replica deletion creates imbalanced placement, hot spots, and unnecessary network traffic, then proposes heterogeneity-aware replica deletion that places more remaining replicas on stronger nodes. On a 23-node cluster, HaRD reduces execution time by up to 60% versus default Hadoop and 17% versus a distribution-aware baseline.

title: A Performance Evaluation and Examination of Open-Source Erasure Coding Libraries For Storage
abstract: This FAST paper benchmarks five open-source erasure coding libraries in encoding and decoding scenarios and studies how implementation choices and parameter settings affect real performance. It is useful for the diploma primarily as an evaluation-methodology source: it identifies coding throughput, decoding behavior, and memory hierarchy effects as practical metrics that can materially change conclusions about a scheme. The paper also explains why theory alone is not enough when comparing coding approaches in storage systems.
